Trust as Infrastructure

January 20, 2026

Trust isn't a feeling. It's a system. And like all systems, it can be designed, maintained, and broken.

Every high-performing team I've been part of had one thing in common: you could say the uncomfortable thing.

Not because everyone was fearless. Because the trust infrastructure was built well enough that the cost of honesty was low and the cost of silence was high.

The equation

I think about trust as:

Trust = Competence × Intent

Competence means: do you know what you're doing, and do you do what you say?

Intent means: do I believe you want good outcomes for me and for this? Are you honest even when it's inconvenient?

Both legs of the equation matter. A highly competent person with bad intent is dangerous. A well-meaning person with no competence is just exhausting. You need both.

Reliability is underrated

In Silicon Valley, everyone talks about capability. Can you code? Can you raise? Can you sell?

Nobody talks about reliability. Which is strange, because reliability is what actually drives trust compound interest.

Showing up when you said you would. Following through on small things when nobody's watching. Being consistent across contexts — the same person with your boss as with your team.

Reliability is the unsexy moat. It's hard to see on a resume. It shows up everywhere in real collaboration.

Breaking it

Trust is asymmetric. It takes a long time to build and a short time to destroy.

This isn't a design flaw — it's a feature. High-trust relationships are worth protecting. The asymmetry makes people think twice before acting in bad faith.

What breaks trust:

  • Saying one thing and doing another, even once
  • Letting people find out things they should have heard from you
  • Prioritizing comfort over honesty in important moments
  • Tolerating behavior that violates the group's values

That last one is underappreciated. Every time a leader tolerates bad behavior from a high performer, they signal that the rules are conditional. That signal spreads.

Rebuilding it

Trust can be repaired. But it requires a path to redemption — visible, sustained change. Not just words.

People are flawed. Relationships depend on forgiveness. But forgiveness doesn't mean ignoring the pattern. It means: I believe you're trying, and I'm watching for evidence.

The organizations that survive long-term have figured out how to hold this tension — high accountability without becoming vindictive, forgiveness without losing standards.

Building trust infrastructure

If you're building a team, trust isn't just about relationships. It's about systems.

How do decisions get made transparently? How does feedback flow? What are the explicit values, and how do they get enforced? Who has permission to say the hard thing?

These are design choices. And like all design choices, not making them is also a choice.

Build the infrastructure first. The relationships follow.

trustrelationshipssystems

Shant Marootian · 2026
